content.full_text:
January 10, 2016


Thomas Brown
St. Helena City Attorney
Burke Williams & Sorensen, LLP
1901 Harrison Street, Suite 900
Oakland, CA 94612-3501

Re:	Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-245

Dear Mr. Brown:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of St. Helena Vice Mayor Peter White regarding his duties under the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Section 1090. Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) [ FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. Does Section 87100 of the Act require the Vice-Mayor to refrain from participating in discussions or decisions regarding development of and/or award of a contract for development of the Adams Street parcel?

2. Does Section 1090 preclude the Vice-Mayor from participating in decisions to award a contract to Bald Mountain and Alcion Ventures for development of the Adams Street parcel, and also prohibit the City from awarding the contract?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

Our File No. A-16-245
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Section 87100 prohibits the Vice-Mayor from making, participating in making or
influencing decisions regarding development of and/or award of a contract for development of the
Adams Street parcel.

2. Section 1090 precludes the Vice-Mayor and the City from awarding a contract to Bald
Mountain and Alcion Ventures for development of the Adams Street parcel.

FACTS

The Vice-Mayor was hired by Eames Construction³ (hereinafter "Eames") on September 7,
2016 as an independent contractor. In this capacity, he serves as a project manager for two Eames
projects, both located outside of St. Helena. He is compensated on an hourly, per-project basis by
Eames. On January 3, 2016, you stated that the Vice-Mayor is a (1) licensed Real Estate Broker,
(2) General Contractor, (3) holds certificates as a Qualified Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan
Practitioner and (3) Qualified Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan Developer.

On June 21, 2016, St. Helena issued an RFP for development of the Adams Street parcel.
On August 19, 2016, the City received three proposals in response to its RFP. One of the proposals
was submitted by Bald Mountain and Alcion Ventures. Bald Mountain and Alcion Ventures'
proposal lists as one of its "team members," Eames and Ryan Eames as a "construction cost
advisor." The proposal provides no further information regarding the capacity in which Eames
would be used should the proposal be selected. You also stated the following:

• The Vice-Mayor has no employment, professional or contractor relationship with Bald
Mountain Development or Alcion Ventures.

• The work that the Vice-Mayor has been engaged to conduct for Eames is in no way related
to Bald Mountain Development or Alcion Ventures. These entities are not partners,
consultants or teammates on either of the two Eames development projects for which the
Vice-Mayor is the project manager.

• Furthermore, the two Eames projects for which the Vice-Mayor is serving as project
manager in no way impact or involve the Adams Street parcel as the projects are located in
Calistoga and Sonoma. Eames has not asked the Vice-Mayor to consult in any manner on
the Adams Street proposal.

• The Vice-Mayor requested your office's advice when he learned, in late September 2016,
that Eames was listed as a "team member" on Bald Mountain and Alcion Ventures proposal
in response to the RFP for development of the Adams Street parcel.

³ Eames is a General Building Contractor according to the Contractors State License Board.

Our File No. A-16-245
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

Political Reform Act

Under Section 87100, a public official may not make, participate in making, or use his or
her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
one or more of the public official's interests.

Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. Based upon
the facts provided, the Vice Mayor has an interest in Eames as a source of income. (Section
87103(c).) Since he has a financial interest in Eames, he would be prohibited from making,
participating in making, or using his official position to influence a governmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on Eames.³

Foreseeability and Materiality: A financial effect on an official's interest is presumed to be
reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a decision
before the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) You stated that the Bald Mountain and Alcion
Ventures application names Eames as one of its "team members." Thus, Eames is a named party (or
co-party as a team member) and is considered explicitly involved.

With respect to a source of income to a public official, Regulation 18702.3(a) states:

"[T]he financial effect is material if:

"(1) The source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or
is otherwise named or identified as the subject of the proceeding . . .."

Again, since Eames is a named applicant, materially would be presumed. Therefore, the
Vice Mayor could not make, participate in making or influence the decision.

Section 1090

In addition to the Act, we must also determine whether Vice Mayor White has a potentially
disqualifying financial interest in decisions regarding contracts between the City and Bald
Mountain and Alcion Ventures under Section 1090 and whether the city itself would be prohibited
from making the contract.

Generally, Section 1090 prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Under this section, "the prohibited

³ In your letter you state that "Although Eames will receive a financial benefit if Bald Mountain/Alcion
Ventures is awarded the Adams Street parcel contract, the possibility that this benefit would flow to the Vice-Mayor is
remote." However, since Eames is the financial interest, the question is not whether the Vice Mayor will be financially
affected, but whether Eames will be financially affected as discussed further below.

Our File No. A-16-243
Page No. 4

act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig
(1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call
(1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract
are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-649.)

It is not contested that Vice Mayor White is a public officer subject to the provisions of
Section 1090 and that the City's decision involves a contract. Moreover, as a member of the City
Council, Vice Mayor White is presumed to be involved in the making of all contracts by the City
irrespective of whether he actually participates in the making of the contract. (Thomson, supra, at
pp. 645, 649.) Thus, the determinative question is whether Vice Mayor White has a financial
interest in the contracts under Section 1090.

As you note, the Attorney General's Office has analyzed facts similar to yours. They were
asked if Section 1090 applied to a city council member who was associated, as an independent
contractor, with a public-relations firm that provided services to two nonprofit organizations that
had contracts with the city.

"In this case, we must determine whether the council member's
association, as an independent contractor, with a public relations firm that
provides some media relations services to two nonprofit agencies contracting with
Glendale—although the member provides no services for, and receives no
compensation from, the nonprofits whatsoever—necessarily confers upon her a
prohibited financial interest in Glendale's contracts with the firm. Based on the
facts as represented here, we conclude that it does not (although we caution that
any change in circumstances might require a different analysis)." (2015 Cal.AG
LEXIS 11.)

However, these facts differ from yours in that the officer's interest in the opinion was in a business
entity not connected to the contracts in question. In your case Eames is a named party in the
application which both creates a greater potential and appearance of conflict of interest. Therefore,
we conclude that the Vice Mayor does have a financial interest in the decision.

Exceptions and the rule of necessity apply.

The Legislature has expressly defined certain "remote interests" or "noninterests" that do
not come within Section 1090's general prohibition. However, none of these exceptions apply to the
facts presented.

In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a
contract that Section 1090 would otherwise prohibit. (Federal Construction Co. v. Curd, 179 Cal.
489, 493 (1918); 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 110 (2005); 65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 310 (1982).)
The rule of necessity allows a public officer or an agency's governing body to carry out the
essential duties of the office or body despite the existence of a conflict of interest where the officer
or body is the only one legally authorized to act, ensuring that essential government functions are
performed even where a conflict of interest exists. However, there is no necessity that Bald

Our File No. A-16-245
Page No. 5

Mountain and Alcion Ventures is awarded the contract with Eames serving as a construction cost advisor, thus the exception does not apply.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl
